metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Tighten the concurrency AC to require real OS-level processes (e.g., `subprocess.Popen` or `multiprocessing.Process`) rather than threads, since `fcntl.flock` is per-process and a thread-based implementation would give a false-green on the 'non-torn markers' assertion.
findings.finding: The concurrency AC requires 'markers written atomically, non-torn state' using 'two processes sweeping the same checkout', but does not mandate real OS-level processes. The existing test suite's pattern uses threads (ThreadPoolExecutor/Thread), and on Linux `fcntl.flock` is per-process — threads in the same process share the lock, so a thread-based test trivially satisfies the assertion without exercising the atomicity guarantee at all, yielding a false-green.
findings.suggested_fix: Amend the AC to: 'Concurrency test: two OS-level processes (e.g., `multiprocessing.Process` or `subprocess.Popen`) sweeping the same checkout converge with atomic, non-torn markers — implemented with real subprocesses, not threads, to exercise `fcntl.flock` cross-process serialization.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a measurable observable to the absent-marker AC for the 'no re-commit storm' property, e.g.: 'calling `run_ensures` N≥3 times on a store with an absent/garbage marker produces at most 1 new commit across all N calls (subsequent calls are idempotent).'
findings.finding: The absent/garbage-marker AC says 'missing/garbage marker degrades to empty-set without crashing' and the What section claims 'no re-commit storm', but no observable bound is specified for 'no storm'. A test that calls `run_ensures` once and asserts it doesn't crash satisfies the AC verbatim while completely skipping the storm-prevention property.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — no_mutate + absent-marker AC; What section ('no re-commit storm')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the exact environment variable that suppresses the staleness hint (e.g., `REBAR_NO_STALE_HINT`) in the AC or a 'Symbol Map' section so the test can be written without guessing.
findings.finding: The staleness-hint AC says 'env var suppresses' without naming the environment variable. There is no matching env-var symbol anywhere in `src/` (no `REBAR_NO_STALE*` or equivalent). The test author has no authoritative name to write the suppression test against.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Staleness-hint tests: 'env var suppresses'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific environment variable in the staleness-hint AC (e.g. `ENSURE_NO_STALENESS_HINT=1`) so the test can assert the correct symbol.
findings.finding: The staleness-hint AC says 'env var suppresses' without naming the environment variable; an executor has no authoritative name to write the test against and must guess or search WS1-3 artefacts.
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'env var suppresses' to 'ENSURE_STALENESS_SUPPRESS=1 (or the name defined in WS1) suppresses the hint' to pin the identifier.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly require the concurrency test to spawn real OS subprocesses (not threads or mocks) so that filesystem-level atomicity (e.g. rename atomicity, lock semantics) is actually exercised at the live boundary.
findings.finding: The concurrency AC ('two processes sweeping the same checkout converge with atomic, non-torn markers') relies on OS-level process isolation and filesystem atomicity, but the plan does not explicitly require testing with real spawned subprocesses rather than threads or mocks. A thread-based or mock-based concurrency test would bypass the real atomicity boundary (file locks, rename atomicity) and could pass green while the live multi-process scenario is broken.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the concurrency AC: 'implemented by spawning two independent subprocesses (e.g. via subprocess.Popen or pytest-xdist workers) sharing the same working directory, verifying atomic marker state via filesystem inspection after both complete.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Sharpen the concurrency AC to state the observable terminal state, e.g. 'exactly one commit is added, both processes read identical marker content afterwards, and no partial/torn file is present on disk.'
findings.finding: The concurrency AC uses 'converge' as the terminal success condition without defining the expected final state (e.g., identical marker content, exactly one commit written, no duplicate commits); a test author cannot write a deterministic assertion without this.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'converge with atomic, non-torn markers' with 'produce exactly one new commit total (no duplicates), leave an identical, well-formed marker file readable by both processes, and leave no zero-byte or partial marker on disk.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Ground the 'no git-config subprocess' assertion by either naming the subprocess spy/mock mechanism to use (e.g. `monkeypatch.setattr(subprocess, 'run', ...)`) or replacing it with a positively-checkable proxy (e.g. assert `run_ensures` is not called on the write path instead).
findings.finding: The hot-path regression AC asserts 'spawns no git-config subprocess' as an empirically-checkable invariant, but there is no named subprocess spy, mock hook, or existing call site for 'git-config' in the codebase that grounds this claim — making it unclear how the test will detect or assert the absence of a git-config subprocess call.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Hot-path regression test AC

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact target test file path(s) to be created (e.g., `tests/unit/test_store_ensures_ws5.py`) to avoid collision with the existing `tests/unit/test_code_review_ws5.py` and give the implementation a clear file-system target.
findings.finding: `test_code_review_ws5.py` already exists in `tests/unit/` and covers a completely different WS5 (code-review security detectors / epic b744). The plan does not name a target test file, creating a risk of a naming collision or confusion when the new WS5 (store ensures) test file is created.
findings.location: Scope section; What section — 'Add tests covering…'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Files' section listing the new test file, e.g., `tests/unit/test_store_ensures_ws5.py` (or the integration-suite equivalent if live git operations are needed).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Split the staleness-hint AC into four separate checkboxes (one each for: hints-once, rate-limit ≤1/24h, env-var suppression, fresh-worktree silence) so each can be independently verified and checked off.
findings.finding: The staleness-hint AC bundles four independent sub-criteria (hints once, rate-limited ≤1/24h, env-var suppression, fresh-worktree silence) into a single checkbox, making partial completion untrackable and the criterion an implicit epic-of-epics.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the concurrency AC explicitly extends (or delegates to) the existing `two_clones` fixture in `test_concurrency_regression.py` and the `_clean_env` + ThreadPoolExecutor pattern in `test_store_concurrency.py`, rather than introducing a third concurrent-process harness.
findings.finding: The concurrency AC ('two processes sweeping the same checkout converge with atomic, non-torn markers') describes building a harness that is substantially already present: tests/integration/test_store_concurrency.py uses ThreadPoolExecutor with N concurrent same-clone writers and tests/integration/test_concurrency_regression.py provides a two-clone fixture with reconvergence helpers. The plan does not state whether it extends these existing harnesses or creates a new one — an implementer might duplicate the fixture boilerplate (bare-remote, two-clones, _expire_sync_marker, etc.) rather than reusing it.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to the concurrency AC: 'Extend tests/integration/test_concurrency_regression.py using the existing two_clones fixture and _expire_sync_marker helper; same-clone atomic-marker races extend the ThreadPoolExecutor pattern in test_store_concurrency.py.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `tests/interfaces/store/test_tracker_config_e2e.py` as an existing caller of `fsck_cli(..., no_mutate=True)` and specify whether the new test extends, replaces, or complements that coverage.
findings.finding: The `no_mutate` fsck AC overlaps with an existing partial test in `tests/interfaces/store/test_tracker_config_e2e.py` (which already calls `fsck_cli([], repo_root=str(repo), no_mutate=True)`). The plan does not enumerate this existing consumer test, so the new test may duplicate, contradict, or silently regress the existing coverage without knowing it.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'no_mutate + absent-marker tests: read-only fsck mutates nothing'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify and document the exact module paths and public symbols (e.g. `rebar._commands.ensures.run_ensures`, the marker file path, the applied-set reader) that WS1-3 will deliver, and add them to the plan as named import targets so the test author has concrete anchors.
findings.finding: The plan's primary test targets — `run_ensures()`, `applied_set`, and the `.ensure-applied` marker — do not exist anywhere in the codebase (`src/` or `tests/`). These are new symbols that WS1-3 would have produced, but they are absent. The test plan names them as if they are concrete edit targets, yet there is nothing to import or call.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — all five AC lines; What section ('run_ensures', 'applied-set read/process', 'markers written atomically')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Prerequisites / Symbol Map' subsection listing the exact importable paths WS1-3 will expose (e.g., `from rebar._commands.ensures import run_ensures`, marker path constant, applied-set reader), explicitly gating this work item on WS1-3 completion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit precondition/dependency statement to the Scope section (e.g. 'WS5 is blocked on WS1-3 being merged') so the sequencing constraint is captured and schedulers/reviewers cannot miss it.
findings.finding: The plan's test ACs assume `run_ensures()`, `.ensure-applied`, the staleness-hint machinery, the 24h rate-limit, env-var suppression, and fresh-worktree-silent behaviour all exist at test time, but none of these constructs are present in the codebase and the plan contains no explicit 'blocked on WS1-3' sequencing dependency or precondition statement. Per fail-closed protocol, unverifiable premises are a gap.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: 'Precondition: WS1-3 merged. WS5 tests the invariants those workstreams introduce; no test can be written until `run_ensures`, `.ensure-applied`, and the staleness-hint machinery exist.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a 'Verification' section naming the target test file(s) and a concrete proving command (e.g. `pytest tests/test_ws5_invariants.py -v --tb=short`) whose green output constitutes completion evidence.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no proving command, test runner invocation, or file location; there is no mechanism by which a reviewer (or CI) can verify that all five ACs are covered and passing — the union-of-steps cannot reach the stated end-state because no steps are described.
findings.location: Plan body — no 'Steps' or 'How to verify' section exists
findings.suggested_fix: Add a section: '## Verification\n Run `pytest tests/test_ws5_invariants.py -v` — all five test groups must be present and green in CI before this ticket is closed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reference the staleness rate-limit (24 h) and hot-path read-count bound (≤1) from a shared named constant or fixture-parameter in the production/test boundary rather than embedding bare literals in the ACs, so threshold changes propagate automatically.
findings.finding: The staleness-hint rate-limit constant '24h' and the hot-path read-count bound '≤1' are asserted as literal numbers in the ACs without stating whether they are derived from a configurable production constant or independently hardcoded in tests. If the production threshold is later tuned, tests will silently diverge.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'rate-limited to ≤1/24h' and 'reads the applied-set at most once per process'
findings.suggested_fix: Export the rate-limit window (e.g., STALENESS_HINT_RATE_LIMIT_SECONDS) and hot-path budget (e.g., MAX_APPLIED_SET_READS_PER_PROCESS) as named constants from the WS1-3 implementation; reference those constants in both test assertions and AC descriptions (e.g., 'rate-limited to ≤1 per STALENESS_HINT_RATE_LIMIT_SECONDS interval').

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the staleness-hint rate-limit state (timestamp/flag) is created, isolated per test case, and torn down — e.g., inject a configurable clock or write the timestamp to a temp directory scoped to each test.
findings.finding: The staleness-hint rate-limit test relies on persisted rate-limit state (e.g., a last-hint timestamp file or similar) but the plan specifies no mechanism for isolating or resetting that shared state between test runs, risking test-order pollution and false passes/fails.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'Rate-limit state is written to an injectable/configurable path; tests use a per-test temp directory and a frozen/injected clock so the 24h window is deterministically controllable without wall-clock sleeps.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the full lifecycle of the shared checkout fixture in the concurrency test: who creates it (fresh temp dir per run), how subprocesses are launched and joined with a timeout, and how the fixture is cleaned up regardless of subprocess exit status.
findings.finding: The concurrency test spins up two processes against the same shared checkout but the plan defines no CREATE or RETIRE lifecycle for that shared checkout fixture, leaving open whether the fixture is per-test-run, persistent, or cleaned up on failure.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Concurrency test: 'two processes sweeping the same checkout converge'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'The concurrency test creates a fresh temporary git repo per invocation, launches subprocesses with an explicit timeout (e.g., 30 s), and unconditionally removes the temp dir in a teardown step even if a subprocess crashes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale (one sentence per AC group) specifying the intended test tier (unit-with-mocked-git vs. integration-with-real-git) and why it is sufficient — e.g., idempotency and concurrency require a real git worktree (lesson from `test_reconcile_idempotency.py`'s 'false green' note), while hot-path and staleness-hint can be unit-level.
findings.finding: The plan's 'Why' section gives a general rationale for adding tests but provides no positive rationale for the chosen approach-level design decision: which ACs target unit-level (mocked git/subprocess) vs. integration-level (real git repo). The existing codebase has both tiers (e.g., `test_concurrency.py` mocks `snapshot_head`; `test_reconcile_idempotency.py` uses a real git worktree), and the choice affects what bugs each test can catch.
findings.location: Why section; Acceptance Criteria (no tier specified for any AC)

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
